questions.promptHtml: A software development team needs to track work items with custom fields for sprint planning, priority levels, and dependencies between tasks. The solution must integrate with code commits and pull requests.

options.label: GitHub Issues with labels and milestones
GitHub Projects with custom fields and issue linking

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Projects with custom fields and issue linking
questions.extraHtml: GitHub Projects (Projects v2) supports custom fields for any metadata, links to issues/PRs automatically, and provides views (board, table, roadmap). Issues with labels lack custom fields flexibility. Third-party tools lose native GitHub integration. Projects v2 is GitHub's native project management with full customization.

questions.promptHtml: Your company requires that all commit messages follow Conventional Commits format (type: description) with automatic enforcement blocking non-compliant commits.

options.label: GitHub Actions with commitlint validation on pull requests
Manual code review of commit messages

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with commitlint validation on pull requests
questions.extraHtml: GitHub Actions with commitlint runs on PR events, validates commit message format, and blocks merge via status checks. Git hooks are client-side only (bypassable). Manual review doesn't scale and is error-prone. Server-side validation provides enforceable commit message standards.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository contains a monorepo with 20 services. Pull requests should automatically be assigned to code owners based on the files changed, preventing merge without owner approval.

options.label: CODEOWNERS file with path patterns and branch protection requiring owner review

questions.correctLabel: CODEOWNERS file with path patterns and branch protection requiring owner review
questions.extraHtml: CODEOWNERS file maps paths to teams/users, automatically requests reviews when files change, branch protection enforces owner approval. Manual assignment doesn't scale. Actions can assign but don't natively integrate with branch protection. CODEOWNERS is the native ownership and review routing mechanism.

questions.promptHtml: A development workflow requires that feature branches be automatically deleted after pull request merge to prevent repository clutter, but release branches must be preserved.

options.label: Repository settings for automatic branch deletion with protected branch exclusions
GitHub Actions workflow deleting all branches

questions.correctLabel: Repository settings for automatic branch deletion with protected branch exclusions
questions.extraHtml: Repository auto-delete merged branches setting preserves protected branches automatically. Manual cleanup doesn't scale and is forgotten. Workflow-based deletion lacks built-in protected branch awareness. Native auto-delete respects protection rules and reduces clutter.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs to measure code review effectiveness: time to first review, number of review rounds, and reviewer participation across 200 repositories.

options.label: GitHub GraphQL API querying pull request review timeline and aggregating metrics

questions.correctLabel: GitHub GraphQL API querying pull request review timeline and aggregating metrics
questions.extraHtml: GraphQL API provides detailed PR timeline data (reviewRequestedAt, reviewedAt, review counts) for custom analytics. Manual tracking doesn't scale. Insights provides some metrics but GraphQL enables custom KPIs. API access allows building tailored code review analytics dashboards.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must build and test code triggered by pull_request events, but certain paths (/docs, README.md) should not trigger expensive CI builds.

options.label: GitHub Actions paths-ignore filter on pull_request trigger
Run all builds and waste resources

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions paths-ignore filter on pull_request trigger
questions.extraHtml: paths-ignore prevents workflow execution when only specified paths change, optimizing CI costs. Running all builds wastes resources. Manual dispatch removes automation benefits. Path filtering is the built-in CI optimization for monorepos and documentation changes.

questions.promptHtml: Your security policy requires that GitHub personal access tokens expire after 90 days maximum, with automatic enforcement preventing creation of longer-lived tokens.

options.label: Organization security policy setting maximum token lifetime

questions.correctLabel: Organization security policy setting maximum token lifetime
questions.extraHtml: Organization policies enforce maximum token expiration, preventing users from creating longer-lived tokens. Email reminders rely on user compliance. Manual audits are point-in-time. Policy enforcement proactively limits credential lifetime risk at the identity provider level.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Enterprise environment must support custom branding with company logo, color scheme, and custom domain for GitHub Pages sites.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise with custom branding settings and GitHub Pages custom domain
Self-hosted Git server with custom UI

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise with custom branding settings and GitHub Pages custom domain
questions.extraHtml: GitHub Enterprise supports custom logos, colors, navigation customization, and Pages custom domains. GitHub.com org profiles have limited customization. Self-hosted servers require maintaining custom UI. Enterprise provides managed custom branding capabilities.

questions.promptHtml: A CI/CD pipeline must deploy to production only during a specified maintenance window (Saturday 00:00-04:00 UTC) with automated approval workflow outside the window.

options.label: GitHub Actions with schedule triggers and conditional deployment logic based on time

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with schedule triggers and conditional deployment logic based on time
questions.extraHtml: Actions can evaluate current time, conditionally run deployment steps during window, or wait/require approval outside window. Manual deployment risks errors. Disabling workflows loses flexibility. Time-based conditionals enable maintenance window enforcement in automation.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs GitHub Copilot deployed to 500 developers with usage analytics showing code acceptance rates and language distribution.

options.label: GitHub Copilot for Business with organization-level analytics dashboard
GitHub Copilot Individual for each developer

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Copilot for Business with organization-level analytics dashboard
questions.extraHtml: Copilot for Business provides centralized billing, policy management, and usage analytics showing suggestions, acceptances, and languages. Individual licenses lack org-level analytics. No tracking prevents ROI measurement. Business tier is the enterprise AI coding assistant deployment model.

questions.promptHtml: A development team practices trunk-based development with feature flags. Releases must be tagged automatically when version numbers in package.json change on the main branch.

options.label: GitHub Actions detecting version changes and creating releases with git tags
Automated nightly builds without version tracking

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions detecting version changes and creating releases with git tags
questions.extraHtml: Actions workflow compares package.json version on push to main, creates GitHub release and git tag when version increments. Manual tagging is error-prone. Nightly builds don't correlate to versions. Version-driven automated releases align with semantic versioning and trunk-based development.

questions.promptHtml: Your company requires that all GitHub OAuth apps used by developers be pre-approved, with automatic blocking of unapproved third-party app access to organization resources.

options.label: GitHub organization OAuth app policy restricting to approved apps

questions.correctLabel: GitHub organization OAuth app policy restricting to approved apps
questions.extraHtml: OAuth app policy allows org admins to approve/deny apps, preventing unapproved app access to org data. Email policies rely on user compliance. No controls create data exfiltration risk. OAuth app restrictions enforce third-party access governance at the platform level.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository needs to enforce Gitflow workflow with protected master and develop branches, requiring pull requests for all changes and preventing force pushes.

options.label: Branch protection rules on master and develop with require PR and block force push

questions.correctLabel: Branch protection rules on master and develop with require PR and block force push
questions.extraHtml: Branch protection enforces PR requirement, blocks force-push, and can require status checks per branch. Training doesn't enforce compliance. Client hooks are bypassable. Server-side branch protection provides enforceable workflow constraints.

questions.promptHtml: Your CI/CD pipeline builds Docker images with layer caching to optimize build times. Workflow must use previous build layer cache from the container registry.

options.label: GitHub Actions with Docker buildx cache-from and cache-to registry

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with Docker buildx cache-from and cache-to registry
questions.extraHtml: Docker buildx supports external cache backends (registry, GitHub cache), reusing layers across workflow runs and reducing build time. No caching wastes time. Manual caching doesn't work in ephemeral runners. Registry-backed layer caching optimizes CI container builds.

questions.promptHtml: A software project needs contributor license agreements (CLA) signed before accepting pull requests, with automated validation blocking merge until CLA is executed.

options.label: CLA bot GitHub App with branch protection requiring CLA check

questions.correctLabel: CLA bot GitHub App with branch protection requiring CLA check
questions.extraHtml: CLA Assistant or similar apps automate signature collection, validate contributor status, report via PR check linked to branch protection. Manual tracking doesn't scale. No enforcement creates legal risk. Automated CLA validation provides IP compliance at code contribution time.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires all production deployments be immutable with rollback capability, preserving previous application versions and infrastructure state.

options.label: GitHub Actions with container immutable tags and IaC state versioning

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with container immutable tags and IaC state versioning
questions.extraHtml: Immutable deployments use unique image tags (e.g., git SHA), IaC state is versioned in remote backend enabling rollback. In-place updates prevent rollback. Manual backups are error-prone. Immutable deployments + versioned state enable reliable rollback and audit trail.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository contains sensitive documentation that should only be accessible to internal employees but the code itself can be public.

options.label: Public repository with private wiki for documentation
Public repository with documentation in separate private repository

questions.correctLabel: Public repository with documentation in separate private repository
questions.extraHtml: GitHub wikis inherit repository visibility (public wiki for public repo). Private repo limits code sharing unnecessarily. Separate private repo for sensitive docs + public repo for code provides granular access control. Repository splitting enables mixed visibility scenarios.

questions.promptHtml: Your development workflow requires preview deployments for every pull request with automatic cleanup when PR is closed, minimizing cloud costs.

options.label: GitHub Actions creating preview environment per PR, cleanup on close event
Shared staging environment overwritten per PR

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions creating preview environment per PR, cleanup on close event
questions.extraHtml: PR opened event triggers preview deployment, closed event triggers cleanup (IaC destroy). Shared staging causes conflicts. Manual provisioning doesn't scale. Ephemeral per-PR environments enable isolated testing with automatic resource cleanup.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub organization has 100 repositories with inconsistent CI/CD practices. Management wants to standardize linting, testing, and security scanning across all repos.

options.label: Organization-level required workflows applied to all repositories
Copy-paste workflow files to each repository

questions.correctLabel: Organization-level required workflows applied to all repositories
questions.extraHtml: Required workflows (org-level) automatically run on all repos, enforce standardization centrally, update once and apply everywhere. Copy-paste creates maintenance burden. Documentation does not enforce compliance. Required workflows provide scalable CI/CD governance.

questions.promptHtml: Your company needs GitHub audit logs exported to AWS S3 for long-term retention with encryption at rest and lifecycle policies archiving to Glacier after 90 days.

options.label: GitHub audit log streaming to S3 with SSE and lifecycle rules

questions.correctLabel: GitHub audit log streaming to S3 with SSE and lifecycle rules
questions.extraHtml: Audit log streaming pushes events to S3, SSE-S3/SSE-KMS provides encryption, S3 lifecycle transitions to Glacier for cost optimization. Manual downloads don't scale. GitHub retention is limited. Streaming + S3 lifecycle enables compliant long-term audit log archival.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow requires credentials to deploy to Kubernetes cluster without storing kubeconfig files in repository or as GitHub secrets.

options.label: GitHub OIDC provider with Kubernetes service account token projection

questions.correctLabel: GitHub OIDC provider with Kubernetes service account token projection
questions.extraHtml: GitHub OIDC provides JWT tokens, Kubernetes can validate and issue short-lived tokens eliminating static credentials. Storing kubeconfig is less secure (rotation challenges). Embedding in YAML exposes credentials in version control. OIDC federation eliminates static Kubernetes credentials.

questions.promptHtml: Your development team needs to create draft releases automatically when code is pushed to release/* branches, with release notes generated from commit messages.

options.label: GitHub Actions creating draft releases with auto-generated release notes

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions creating draft releases with auto-generated release notes
questions.extraHtml: Actions workflow on branch push creates draft release via GitHub API, auto-generated release notes pull commit messages and PR titles. Manual notes are time-consuming. No documentation hinders adoption. Automated draft releases streamline version management and changelog generation.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository has 10,000 open issues. Management needs to automatically close stale issues with no activity for 90 days after a 14-day warning period.

options.label: Stale bot GitHub Action with configured timeframes

questions.correctLabel: Stale bot GitHub Action with configured timeframes
questions.extraHtml: Stale Action labels inactive issues, comments with warning, closes after grace period if no response. Manual triage doesn't scale (10K issues). Bulk close loses signal. Automated stale issue management maintains repository health with user notification.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires fine-grained audit logs showing who accessed which repository, when, and from what IP address for security investigations.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise audit log with repository access events and IP logging

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise audit log with repository access events and IP logging
questions.extraHtml: Enterprise audit log captures repository access events with actor, timestamp, IP address, and action details queryable via API or streaming. Server logs miss application-level actions. No tracking prevents incident investigation. Audit log is the authoritative source for access forensics.

questions.promptHtml: A CI/CD workflow must execute different build steps based on git branch: feature/* runs tests only, develop runs tests and deploys to staging, main deploys to production.

options.label: GitHub Actions with conditional job execution using github.ref or branch filters

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with conditional job execution using github.ref or branch filters
questions.extraHtml: if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' conditionally runs jobs, or on.push.branches filters trigger workflows per branch pattern. Separate workflows duplicate logic. Single undifferentiated workflow lacks environment isolation. Conditional execution provides branch-aware CI/CD logic.

questions.promptHtml: Your security team needs GitHub Advanced Security alerts aggregated across 500 repositories with daily email reports summarizing new vulnerabilities by severity.

options.label: GitHub GraphQL API querying organization security alerts with scheduled report generation
Manually check each repository security tab

questions.correctLabel: GitHub GraphQL API querying organization security alerts with scheduled report generation
questions.extraHtml: GraphQL API aggregates Dependabot, CodeQL, and secret scanning alerts across orgs, enables custom reporting (daily summaries, severity breakdown). Manual checking does not scale (500 repos). Per-repo emails create noise. Centralized API-driven reporting provides actionable security visibility.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository tracks bugs, features, and documentation tasks. Work items should be automatically categorized and prioritized based on labels with visualization in project boards.

options.label: GitHub Projects automation with label-based field updates and views

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Projects automation with label-based field updates and views
questions.extraHtml: Projects v2 workflows automatically set custom fields (priority, type) based on issue labels, filtered views show categorized work. Manual updates don't scale. No PM loses visibility. Label-driven project automation provides organized work tracking with minimal manual effort.

questions.promptHtml: Your company requires that all pushes to production branches trigger security scans, and detected vulnerabilities automatically create Jira tickets assigned to security team.

options.label: GitHub Actions with security scanning and Jira API integration on push events

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with security scanning and Jira API integration on push events
questions.extraHtml: Push-triggered Actions run security tools (Trivy, Snyk), parse results, create Jira issues via API with vulnerability details. Manual reviews lag behind commits. Weekly scans delay detection. Real-time push-based scanning + ticketing provides immediate security feedback loop.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must authenticate to Azure using federated credentials without storing any client secrets, with permissions scoped to specific resource groups.

options.label: GitHub OIDC provider with Azure AD federated identity and RBAC assignments
Service principal credentials in GitHub secrets

questions.correctLabel: GitHub OIDC provider with Azure AD federated identity and RBAC assignments
questions.extraHtml: GitHub OIDC issues tokens, Azure AD trusts GitHub as identity provider, service principal uses federated credential (no secret), RBAC limits permissions. Stored secrets have rotation challenges. Shared accounts lack audit trail. Federated identity + RBAC provides secretless, scoped Azure access.

questions.promptHtml: Your development team needs pull request templates enforcing checklist items: tests updated, documentation updated, breaking changes documented.
questions.correctLabel: .github/pull_request_template.md with markdown checklist
questions.extraHtml: PR template file automatically populates PR description with checklist, ensuring consistent information and review criteria. Wiki requires manual lookup. Email guidelines are ignored. Templates provide structured, enforceable PR information collection.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Enterprise Server instance needs to support air-gapped deployment in a secure facility with no internet access, including package installation and GitHub Actions runner functionality.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise Server offline installation with pre-downloaded actions and self-hosted runners

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise Server offline installation with pre-downloaded actions and self-hosted runners
questions.extraHtml: GHES supports offline installation via package download, self-hosted runners don't need internet, Actions can use local actions repositories. Cloud requires internet. Internet access violates air-gap requirement. GHES offline + local actions enables secure air-gapped SCM.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs to prevent repository forks from being created to avoid code leakage, while still allowing internal collaboration on repositories.

options.label: Organization settings disabling fork creation for private repositories

questions.correctLabel: Organization settings disabling fork creation for private repositories
questions.extraHtml: Org policy controls fork creation by repository visibility (allow public, block private). Permission restrictions don't prevent forking. Training doesn't enforce. Fork policy prevents private code distribution via forks while enabling public OSS contribution workflows.

questions.promptHtml: A CI/CD pipeline must execute integration tests requiring three microservices running simultaneously with service discovery and networking.

options.label: GitHub Actions service containers with network aliases

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions service containers with network aliases
questions.extraHtml: Service containers run in same Docker network, accessible via service name as hostname, enabling realistic integration testing. External environments add complexity. Mocks don't catch integration issues. Service containers provide isolated, realistic multi-service testing in CI.

questions.promptHtml: Your company requires notification when any repository in the organization is made public, with automatic reversion to private within 5 minutes.

options.label: GitHub webhook for repository visibility changes with automated remediation workflow

questions.correctLabel: GitHub webhook for repository visibility changes with automated remediation workflow
questions.extraHtml: Repository webhook emits event on visibility change, automation (Action or external) reverts to private via API within minutes. Daily audit has large detection window. User awareness doesn't prevent accidents. Real-time webhook-based monitoring + automated response prevents data exposure.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must publish npm packages to GitHub Packages registry with version validation preventing accidental republishing of existing versions.

options.label: GitHub Actions with npm publish, GITHUB_TOKEN authentication, and version check script

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with npm publish, GITHUB_TOKEN authentication, and version check script
questions.extraHtml: Actions publish workflow uses GITHUB_TOKEN (auto-provided, scoped), script checks existing versions in registry before publish preventing overwrites. No validation causes failures. Manual publish lacks automation benefits. Version-aware automated publishing prevents registry conflicts.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs to enforce that all GitHub repositories have README, LICENSE, and SECURITY.md files with automated validation blocking repository creation without these files.

options.label: Repository templates with required files and organization policies

questions.correctLabel: Repository templates with required files and organization policies
questions.extraHtml: Repository templates include required files, combined with org policies and repository rulesets can enforce file presence. Guidelines don't enforce. Manual audits are reactive. Templates + policies proactively ensure repository documentation standards.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must deploy to multiple environments (dev, staging, production) in sequence with manual approval gates between staging and production.

options.label: GitHub Actions with environment-based deployments and required reviewers on production

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with environment-based deployments and required reviewers on production
questions.extraHtml: GitHub Environments define deployment order, required reviewers pause workflow for approval, environment secrets scope credentials. Single workflow without gates lacks safety. Separate workflows lose orchestration. Environment-based deployment provides staged rollout with human checkpoints.

questions.promptHtml: Your security policy requires scanning GitHub Actions workflows for security issues: use of deprecated actions, credential exposure risks, and privilege escalation patterns.

options.label: Workflow security scanning with actionlint or third-party tools in CI

questions.correctLabel: Workflow security scanning with actionlint or third-party tools in CI
questions.extraHtml: Actionlint validates workflow syntax and security practices, can be integrated into PR checks. Manual review doesn't scale and misses subtle issues. No validation creates security risk. Automated workflow scanning provides CI/CD pipeline security assurance.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository needs different issue templates for bugs, feature requests, and security vulnerabilities with custom fields specific to each type.

options.label: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ folder with multiple YAML issue form templates

questions.correctLabel: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ folder with multiple YAML issue form templates
questions.extraHtml: Issue forms support custom fields (dropdowns, checkboxes, text), multiple templates provide type-specific structure. Generic template lacks structure. No templates result in incomplete issue reports. Multiple issue forms ensure complete, structured information collection per issue type.

questions.promptHtml: Your company requires that GitHub Actions workflows only use actions from GitHub-verified creators or internal organization actions, blocking all other third-party actions.

options.label: Organization action usage policy allowing verified and local actions only

questions.correctLabel: Organization action usage policy allowing verified and local actions only
questions.extraHtml: Action usage policies specify allowed action sources (verified creators, same org, specific repos), enforced at workflow execution time. Manual audits don't prevent usage. No restrictions create supply chain risk. Usage policies provide enforceable action allowlisting.

questions.promptHtml: A development team uses GitHub Discussions for Q&A, RFCs, and knowledge sharing. Popular discussions should be automatically converted to pinned wiki pages.

options.label: GitHub Actions with Discussions GraphQL API and wiki update automation

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with Discussions GraphQL API and wiki update automation
questions.extraHtml: Actions workflow queries Discussions via GraphQL (by votes/activity), formats content as wiki markdown, updates wiki via git push. Manual maintenance doesn't scale. Discussions-only loses curated knowledge. Automated discussion-to-wiki pipeline creates living documentation from community knowledge.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires bi-directional synchronization between GitHub Issues and Jira tickets with real-time updates propagating in both directions.

options.label: GitHub webhook + Jira webhook with bidirectional sync service or integration app
Manual ticket creation in both systems

questions.correctLabel: GitHub webhook + Jira webhook with bidirectional sync service or integration app
questions.extraHtml: Webhooks from both platforms trigger sync service updating the other platform real-time, maintaining bidirectional state. Manual duplication causes drift. Batch sync creates lag. Webhook-driven bidirectional integration provides real-time cross-platform work item synchronization.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must execute expensive end-to-end tests only when specific code paths change, not for documentation or configuration changes.

options.label: GitHub Actions paths filter triggering E2E tests only for source code paths
Run all tests on every push

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions paths filter triggering E2E tests only for source code paths
questions.extraHtml: on.push.paths: ['src/**', 'tests/**'] triggers expensive tests only when relevant code changes, docs/config changes skip. Always running wastes resources. Manual triggering loses automation. Path-based selective testing optimizes CI resource utilization.

questions.promptHtml: Your company requires GitHub Enterprise audit logs be retained for 10 years in immutable storage for compliance, with efficient querying capabilities.

options.label: Audit log streaming to Azure Blob with immutability policy and indexed in Azure Data Lake

questions.correctLabel: Audit log streaming to Azure Blob with immutability policy and indexed in Azure Data Lake
questions.extraHtml: Stream audit logs to Blob storage with legal hold/time-based retention preventing deletion, Azure Data Lake analytics enables efficient querying. Local retention has limits. No long-term storage violates compliance. Immutable cloud storage + analytics meets regulatory requirements.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub organization has teams with nested membership (engineering > frontend > react-team). Repository permissions should automatically inherit from parent team hierarchy.

options.label: Nested teams with inherited repository access
Flat teams with manual permission replication

questions.correctLabel: Nested teams with inherited repository access
questions.extraHtml: Nested teams inherit parent permissions automatically, child teams add permissions without removing parent access. Flat teams require duplicate permission grants. Individual permissions don't scale. Team nesting provides hierarchical, inherited access control mirroring org structure.

questions.promptHtml: Your development workflow requires that pull requests from external contributors be reviewed and approved before workflow execution to prevent malicious code in Actions.

options.label: Branch protection requiring pull request reviews and first-time contributor workflow approval
Allow all workflows to run automatically

questions.correctLabel: Branch protection requiring pull request reviews and first-time contributor workflow approval
questions.extraHtml: Public repos require first-time contributor workflow approval by maintainer before Actions run, reviewers verify code safety. Auto-run creates risk (crypto mining, credential theft). Disabling contributions harms OSS. Approval gates balance security with open collaboration.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Enterprise Server deployment must support LDAP authentication integration with nested group membership and automatic team synchronization.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise Server LDAP Sync with nested group support

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise Server LDAP Sync with nested group support
questions.extraHtml: GHES LDAP Sync automatically provisions users, synchronizes team membership based on LDAP groups including nested groups. Manual management doesn't scale and creates drift. GitHub.com doesn't support LDAP. LDAP Sync provides automated identity and access lifecycle from enterprise directory.

questions.promptHtml: Your CI/CD pipeline must build multi-architecture container images (amd64, arm64) for cross-platform deployment with single image manifest.

options.label: GitHub Actions with Docker buildx multi-platform builds and manifest lists

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with Docker buildx multi-platform builds and manifest lists
questions.extraHtml: Docker buildx builds multiple architectures in one command, creates manifest list enabling single tag for all platforms. Separate builds create management complexity. Single arch limits deployment targets. Multi-platform buildx enables universal container images.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository must enforce rebase-only merges to maintain linear history, preventing merge commits and squashing.

options.label: Branch protection allowing rebase merge only, disabling merge commit and squash

questions.correctLabel: Branch protection allowing rebase merge only, disabling merge commit and squash
questions.extraHtml: Repository merge button settings specify allowed merge types, branch protection enforces. Guidelines don't enforce behavior. Pre-receive hooks are complex. Native merge type restrictions provide simple, enforceable history linearity.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs to track technical debt by labeling issues/PRs with debt categories and measure debt accumulation over time with visualization.

options.label: GitHub Issues with custom labels and Projects tracking debt metrics via GraphQL API

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Issues with custom labels and Projects tracking debt metrics via GraphQL API
questions.extraHtml: Label issues with technical-debt labels (security, performance, refactor), Projects aggregate by label, GraphQL API exports time-series data for charting. Spreadsheets create duplication. No tracking prevents debt management. Native GitHub features + API enables integrated debt visibility.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must provision cloud infrastructure using Terraform with diff preview in PR comments before applying changes.

options.label: GitHub Actions with Terraform plan, post plan diff to PR comments, apply on merge

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with Terraform plan, post plan diff to PR comments, apply on merge
questions.extraHtml: Actions on pull_request runs terraform plan, GitHub bot comments with diff, merge triggers terraform apply. No preview creates surprise changes. Manual IaC defeats automation. Plan-in-PR-comments provides reviewable infrastructure changes as code review.

questions.promptHtml: Your security team requires all repository secrets be rotated every 60 days with automated expiration notifications to repository admins.

options.label: Secret metadata tracking with scheduled workflow checking age and notifying via Issues/Slack

questions.correctLabel: Secret metadata tracking with scheduled workflow checking age and notifying via Issues/Slack
questions.extraHtml: Track secret creation dates (in separate metadata), scheduled Action checks age, creates issue or sends notification when approaching expiration. Manual tracking doesn't scale. No rotation leaves stale credentials. Automated secret age monitoring enables proactive credential lifecycle management.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository uses GitHub Packages to distribute container images. Access to private images should be controlled via fine-grained package permissions separate from repository access.

options.label: GitHub Packages with granular package visibility and permission settings

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Packages with granular package visibility and permission settings
questions.extraHtml: Packages support independent access control (inherit from repo or custom), enabling different teams to access packages vs. code. Repo permissions are too coarse. Public packages expose private artifacts. Granular package permissions provide artifact-level access control.

questions.promptHtml: Your development team needs to automatically assign pull requests to reviewers based on round-robin distribution ensuring workload balance.

options.label: GitHub Actions with reviewer assignment logic using round-robin algorithm and GraphQL API

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with reviewer assignment logic using round-robin algorithm and GraphQL API
questions.extraHtml: Action workflow on PR opened tracks last assignee in storage, assigns next reviewer in rotation via API balancing load. Manual assignment creates uneven distribution. Random assignment doesn't balance workload. Round-robin automation provides fair reviewer load distribution.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Enterprise organization must enforce that all repositories have branch protection enabled on default branch before any code can be pushed.

options.label: Repository rulesets applied at organization level requiring branch protection

questions.correctLabel: Repository rulesets applied at organization level requiring branch protection
questions.extraHtml: Repository rulesets (newer than branch protection) apply rules organization-wide or by pattern, enforced at creation time. Manual config doesn't scale. Post-creation audit allows unprotected period. Rulesets provide proactive, scalable repository policy enforcement.

questions.promptHtml: Your CI/CD pipeline must generate and publish test coverage reports with historical trend tracking and automatic PR comments showing coverage changes.

options.label: GitHub Actions with coverage tool, Codecov/Coveralls integration, and PR comment action

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with coverage tool, Codecov/Coveralls integration, and PR comment action
questions.extraHtml: Actions run coverage tool (jest, pytest), publish to coverage service with trend analysis, bot comments on PR with coverage diff. Local reports lack team visibility. No tracking allows coverage degradation. Integrated coverage reporting provides quality metrics with PR feedback.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub organization needs to provide external auditors read-only access to all repositories and audit logs without granting them user accounts.

options.label: GitHub App with read-only permissions installed at organization level

questions.correctLabel: GitHub App with read-only permissions installed at organization level
questions.extraHtml: GitHub App with contents:read and organization:read permissions provides API access to repos and audit logs without user account (better audit trail, limited permissions). User accounts grant more access than needed. Email exports aren't real-time. App-based access provides secure, scoped external audit capabilities.

questions.promptHtml: Your development workflow requires that all commits include an issue reference (#123) with validation blocking commits missing issue links.

options.label: GitHub Actions validating commit messages and blocking merge via required status checks

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions validating commit messages and blocking merge via required status checks
questions.extraHtml: Action on pull_request parses commits for issue references (#123), fails check if missing, branch protection prevents merge. Guidelines don't enforce. Post-merge audit is reactive. Pre-merge commit validation enforces traceability between code and work items.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository must support multiple deployment environments (dev, staging, production) with environment-specific approval workflows, secrets, and deployment history tracking.

options.label: GitHub Environments with per-environment secrets, required reviewers, and deployment logs

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Environments with per-environment secrets, required reviewers, and deployment logs
questions.extraHtml: Environments provide isolated secrets, reviewer requirements, deployment protection rules, and history per environment. Shared environment lacks separation. External tools duplicate GitHub capabilities. Environments are GitHub's native multi-stage deployment construct.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs GitHub repository settings standardized across 300 repositories with version control and automated consistency checks.

options.label: Infrastructure-as-code for GitHub repositories using Terraform GitHub provider

questions.correctLabel: Infrastructure-as-code for GitHub repositories using Terraform GitHub provider
questions.extraHtml: Terraform GitHub provider manages repos, teams, branch protection as code enabling version control, review, and consistency. Manual config creates drift (300 repos). One-time scripts don't maintain state. Repository-as-code provides versioned, reviewable, consistent repository configuration management.

questions.promptHtml: A CI/CD workflow must parallelize test execution across multiple test suites with failure isolation ensuring one failing suite doesn't block others.

options.label: GitHub Actions matrix strategy with continue-on-error per job

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions matrix strategy with continue-on-error per job
questions.extraHtml: Matrix executes test suites in parallel jobs, continue-on-error: true on individual jobs prevents cascading failures, final step aggregates results. Sequential is slow. Single suite has no isolation. Parallel with failure isolation optimizes test execution speed and feedback.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires that internal GitHub packages not be externally accessible even with authentication, enforcing network-level access control.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise with IP allowlists restricting package registry access

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise with IP allowlists restricting package registry access
questions.extraHtml: IP allowlists enforce network perimeter for package access, even with valid auth tokens access blocked from non-allowed IPs. Visibility alone doesn't restrict network access. No network control allows credential exfiltration. IP allowlists provide defense-in-depth for sensitive packages.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must securely access HashiCorp Vault secrets without storing Vault tokens, using dynamic credential retrieval.

options.label: GitHub OIDC provider with Vault JWT authentication

questions.correctLabel: GitHub OIDC provider with Vault JWT authentication
questions.extraHtml: GitHub OIDC issues JWT, Vault JWT auth validates GitHub identity and issues short-lived Vault token, no static credentials stored. Token in secrets requires rotation. Hardcoding is insecure. OIDC + Vault JWT auth provides secretless secrets management integration.

questions.promptHtml: Your development team needs to enforce code formatting standards automatically, with pull requests blocked if code doesn't match the defined style.

options.label: GitHub Actions with formatter tool (prettier, black) and required style check

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with formatter tool (prettier, black) and required style check
questions.extraHtml: Action runs formatter verification on PR, fails check if formatting doesn't match, branch protection blocks merge. Manual review wastes reviewer time. IDE settings aren't enforced. Automated style checks enforce consistent formatting freeing reviewers for substantive review.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Enterprise deployment needs single sign-on with multi-factor authentication enforced, automatic user provisioning from Okta, and deprovisioning on termination.

options.label: SAML SSO with Okta, SCIM provisioning, and MFA enforced in Okta

questions.correctLabel: SAML SSO with Okta, SCIM provisioning, and MFA enforced in Okta
questions.extraHtml: SAML provides federated auth, Okta enforces MFA, SCIM automates user lifecycle (provision on hire, deprovision on termination). Username/password lacks SSO and automation. Manual management doesn't scale and is slow. SAML+SCIM+MFA provides complete enterprise identity integration.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires GitHub Copilot suggestions be filtered to exclude code matching public repositories to avoid license compliance issues.

options.label: GitHub Copilot with public code filter enabled in organization settings

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Copilot with public code filter enabled in organization settings
questions.extraHtml: Copilot public code filter blocks suggestions matching public code, reducing license risk. No filtering creates compliance exposure. Disabling loses productivity benefits. Public code filtering balances AI-assisted development with license compliance.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository uses submodules for shared libraries. CI/CD workflows must recursively checkout submodules with specific authentication for private submodule repositories.

options.label: GitHub Actions checkout action with submodules: recursive and token parameter

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions checkout action with submodules: recursive and token parameter
questions.extraHtml: Checkout action submodules: recursive fetches all nested submodules, token parameter provides auth for private repos. Manual commands add complexity. No submodule support breaks builds. First-class submodule checkout support enables monorepo patterns with shared libraries.

questions.promptHtml: Your security team needs to identify all GitHub repositories containing specific types of sensitive data (SSN patterns, credit card numbers) across 500 repositories.

options.label: GitHub Advanced Security custom secret scanning patterns at organization level

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Advanced Security custom secret scanning patterns at organization level
questions.extraHtml: Custom secret scanning patterns (regex) detect organization-specific sensitive data, scan across all repos, alert on detection. Manual search doesn't scale (500 repos). No detection leaves data exposure risk. Custom patterns extend secret scanning to domain-specific sensitive data.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow deploys to production with a required manual approval from deployment managers, with approval requests sent via Slack for immediate visibility.

options.label: GitHub Environment with required reviewers and Actions Slack integration for notifications

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Environment with required reviewers and Actions Slack integration for notifications
questions.extraHtml: Environment required reviewers pause deployment, Slack Action sends notification with approval link on deployment trigger. Email lacks immediacy. No approvals create change management risk. Environment approvals + Slack notifications enable timely, controlled production deployments.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires all GitHub Actions workflows be reviewed by security team before execution, with a workflow approval process for new or modified workflows.

options.label: Branch protection on .github/workflows/ path requiring security team review

questions.correctLabel: Branch protection on .github/workflows/ path requiring security team review
questions.extraHtml: CODEOWNERS for .github/workflows/* assigns security team, branch protection requires owner review before merge. Post-merge audit is reactive and workflows already ran. No review creates pipeline security risk. Pre-merge workflow review provides preventive CI/CD security control.

questions.promptHtml: A development project needs dependency version updates automatically proposed weekly with grouped PRs per dependency type (npm, pip, go modules).

options.label: Dependabot with scheduled updates and grouped update strategy

questions.correctLabel: Dependabot with scheduled updates and grouped update strategy
questions.extraHtml: Dependabot schedule interval configures update frequency, groups configuration batches updates by package manager or pattern reducing PR noise. Manual updates lag behind security patches. No updates accumulate vulnerability debt. Scheduled grouped Dependabot provides manageable, regular dependency maintenance.

questions.promptHtml: Your CI/CD pipeline must generate software bill of materials (SBOM) for compliance, tracking all dependencies and their versions in production deployments.

options.label: GitHub Actions with SBOM generation tool (syft, cyclonedx) publishing artifacts

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with SBOM generation tool (syft, cyclonedx) publishing artifacts
questions.extraHtml: Actions workflow generates SBOM (CycloneDX/SPDX format) from build artifacts, uploads as release asset or artifact for compliance/security analysis. Manual docs become stale. No SBOM prevents vulnerability correlation. Automated SBOM generation provides supply chain transparency.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub organization has multiple development teams with separate security requirements. Security policies should be enforced per team with team-specific vulnerability thresholds.

options.label: Repository rulesets scoped to team-owned repository patterns

questions.correctLabel: Repository rulesets scoped to team-owned repository patterns
questions.extraHtml: Rulesets target repositories by naming pattern or team ownership, enabling team-specific policies (security scanning, branch protection). Single policy too broad or too restrictive. No enforcement creates inconsistency. Scoped rulesets provide flexible, team-aligned security governance.

questions.promptHtml: Your company requires that GitHub CLI (gh) be authenticated using device flow for headless CI environments without browser access.

options.label: GitHub CLI device flow authentication with gh auth login --with-token

questions.correctLabel: GitHub CLI device flow authentication with gh auth login --with-token
questions.extraHtml: Device flow displays code for user activation on another device, suitable for headless environments. PAT in env works but less secure than OAuth. Web flow requires browser (unavailable headless). Device flow provides user-friendly headless authentication.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository must enforce conventional commits (type(scope): message) with automatic changelog generation on release from commit messages.

options.label: Commitlint validation in PR checks + semantic-release for automated changelogs

questions.correctLabel: Commitlint validation in PR checks + semantic-release for automated changelogs
questions.extraHtml: Commitlint CI check enforces conventional commits, semantic-release parses commit types (feat, fix) to auto-generate changelogs and determine version bumps. Manual changelogs lag and are error-prone. No standards prevent automation. Conventional commits + semantic-release automate version management.

questions.promptHtml: Your development workflow requires database schema migrations be validated in CI before deployment with automatic rollback scripts generated.

options.label: GitHub Actions with database migration tool (Flyway, Liquibase) dry-run validation

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with database migration tool (Flyway, Liquibase) dry-run validation
questions.extraHtml: Migration tools validate syntax, simulate applying to test database in CI, generate rollback scripts automatically. No validation creates deployment failures. Testing in production is dangerous. CI migration validation catches schema issues before production deployment.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Enterprise organization needs to measure adoption metrics: active users, repository activity, CI/CD usage, and security feature enablement across business units.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise Server/Cloud Admin API and audit logs with custom analytics

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise Server/Cloud Admin API and audit logs with custom analytics
questions.extraHtml: Admin API provides user counts, repository stats, Actions usage, security feature enablement; audit logs track activities. Custom dashboards aggregate per BU. Manual surveys don't capture actual usage. No metrics prevent ROI measurement. API-driven analytics provide data-driven GitHub adoption visibility.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires GPG keys for commit signing be managed centrally with automatic key distribution to developers and revocation on employee departure.

options.label: Organization PKI with automated GPG key provisioning and lifecycle management

questions.correctLabel: Organization PKI with automated GPG key provisioning and lifecycle management
questions.extraHtml: Enterprise PKI issues GPG keys to developers automatically, revokes on termination, integrates with identity provider. Individual management doesn't scale and keys aren't revoked. No signing allows commit impersonation. Centralized GPG key lifecycle enables scalable commit authenticity verification.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must cache build artifacts with automatic cache eviction after 7 days to manage storage costs while maximizing cache hit rates.

options.label: GitHub Actions cache with custom key strategy and TTL awareness

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions cache with custom key strategy and TTL awareness
questions.extraHtml: GitHub cache automatically evicts after 7 days, cache key strategy (hash of dependencies) ensures proper invalidation. No cache wastes time. Permanent cache consumes storage indefinitely. TTL-aware caching with smart key strategy balances performance and storage cost.

questions.promptHtml: Your security policy requires that all production deployments be signed and verifiable, proving provenance from specific GitHub workflows.

options.label: GitHub Actions attestation generation with Sigstore signatures

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions attestation generation with Sigstore signatures
questions.extraHtml: GitHub generates signed build provenance attestations using Sigstore, verifiable later to prove build origin and integrity. Unsigned deployments can't prove authenticity. Manual docs aren't tamper-proof. Cryptographic attestations provide supply chain security with verifiable build provenance.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository has 50 contributors from different time zones. Code review response time must be minimized by assigning reviewers in currently working timezones.

options.label: GitHub Actions with timezone-aware reviewer assignment logic

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with timezone-aware reviewer assignment logic
questions.extraHtml: Action determines PR author timezone, selects reviewers in working hours (or next timezone), assigns via API minimizing review latency. Random assignment ignores availability. Manual coordination doesn't scale. Timezone-aware automation optimizes global team code review responsiveness.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs GitHub repository content backed up to separate storage outside GitHub for disaster recovery with daily incremental backups.

options.label: Automated git mirror with cron job pushing to separate git hosting or object storage

questions.correctLabel: Automated git mirror with cron job pushing to separate git hosting or object storage
questions.extraHtml: Cron job git clones all repos, pushes to backup git server or exports bundle to S3/Blob storage daily. Manual backups create gaps. Single provider dependency creates risk. Automated external backups provide disaster recovery independent of primary SCM platform.

questions.promptHtml: A CI/CD workflow must execute smoke tests on deployed staging environment with automatic rollback if tests fail within 10 minutes post-deployment.

options.label: GitHub Actions with deployment, test job with timeout, conditional rollback step

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with deployment, test job with timeout, conditional rollback step
questions.extraHtml: Workflow deploys staging, runs smoke tests with 10min timeout, if: failure() condition triggers rollback deployment. No testing deploys broken code. Manual testing delays feedback. Automated smoke tests with rollback provide rapid deployment validation and recovery.

questions.promptHtml: Your GitHub organization must enforce maximum repository size limits preventing repositories from exceeding 5GB to manage storage costs and performance.

options.label: Pre-receive hooks or repository rulesets checking repository size on push

questions.correctLabel: Pre-receive hooks or repository rulesets checking repository size on push
questions.extraHtml: Pre-receive hooks reject pushes exceeding size threshold (GHES), rulesets provide similar capability. No limits allow runaway storage. Manual monitoring is reactive. Proactive size enforcement prevents performance degradation and cost overruns from oversized repositories.

questions.promptHtml: A development team needs inline code suggestions during development with context-aware completions based on repository codebase and organizational patterns.

options.label: GitHub Copilot for Business integrated in supported IDEs

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Copilot for Business integrated in supported IDEs
questions.extraHtml: Copilot provides AI-powered code suggestions trained on public code and can index organization repositories for context. Manual snippets lack intelligence. Generic autocomplete doesn't understand semantics. Copilot transforms developer productivity with context-aware AI assistance.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires Git hooks be standardized across all developer workstations with centralized management and automatic updates.

options.label: Git template directory with hooks distributed via organization repository
Email instructions for manual hook installation

questions.correctLabel: Git template directory with hooks distributed via organization repository
questions.extraHtml: Git init.templateDir points to shared hook templates, developers clone template repo, hooks auto-apply on git init/clone. Email instructions aren't followed consistently. No standardization creates inconsistent quality gates. Centralized template distribution enables standardized pre-commit validation.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must deploy Lambda functions to AWS with automatic rollback if CloudWatch alarms trigger indicating errors.

options.label: GitHub Actions with AWS SAM/CDK deploy, CloudWatch alarm polling, conditional rollback

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with AWS SAM/CDK deploy, CloudWatch alarm polling, conditional rollback
questions.extraHtml: Workflow deploys Lambda, waits for CloudWatch alarm evaluation period, queries alarm state, triggers rollback deployment if alarm fires. No monitoring deploys broken functions. Manual decisions delay recovery. Automated health-check-based rollback provides safe serverless deployments.

questions.promptHtml: Your company needs all GitHub repository changes logged in external SIEM system for security correlation with other enterprise events.

options.label: GitHub audit log streaming to SIEM via webhook or API with normalized schema

questions.correctLabel: GitHub audit log streaming to SIEM via webhook or API with normalized schema
questions.extraHtml: Audit log streaming delivers events to SIEM (Splunk, Sentinel) enabling correlation with endpoint, network, and identity events. Manual exports lack timeliness. Isolated GitHub logs miss cross-system attacks. Integrated audit streaming enables holistic security monitoring.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository must support semantic versioning with automatic version bumps based on conventional commit types (feat=minor, fix=patch, BREAKING CHANGE=major).

options.label: semantic-release tool in GitHub Actions parsing commits and managing versions

questions.correctLabel: semantic-release tool in GitHub Actions parsing commits and managing versions
questions.extraHtml: semantic-release analyzes commit history, determines version bump per SemVer rules, updates package version, creates git tag, and publishes release. Manual versioning is error-prone. Random versions violate SemVer contracts. Automated semantic versioning ensures consistent, meaningful version management.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs GitHub Enterprise Server metrics exported to Prometheus for dashboarding and alerting on system health.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise Server management console exporting Prometheus metrics

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise Server management console exporting Prometheus metrics
questions.extraHtml: GHES exposes Prometheus metrics endpoint for scraping (CPU, memory, git operations, Actions queues, storage). Manual log parsing doesn't scale. No metrics prevent proactive issue detection. Native Prometheus metrics enable production-grade GHES observability.

questions.promptHtml: A CI/CD pipeline must build and sign container images with provenance attestations meeting SLSA level 3 requirements for supply chain security.

options.label: GitHub Actions with Docker buildx, OIDC token, Sigstore cosign, and SLSA provenance

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with Docker buildx, OIDC token, Sigstore cosign, and SLSA provenance
questions.extraHtml: GitHub OIDC provides build identity, buildx generates image, cosign signs with keyless signing, SLSA provenance attests build steps. Unsigned images lack authenticity proof. Manual signing doesn't scale. Automated signing + provenance meets supply chain security standards (SLSA).

questions.promptHtml: Your development workflow requires branch protection rules be tested before deploying to production repositories to prevent lockouts.

options.label: Test branch protection on pilot repository, export via API, import to production repos

questions.correctLabel: Test branch protection on pilot repository, export via API, import to production repos
questions.extraHtml: Create test repo, configure protection rules, verify with test PRs, export config via API, replicate to production repos programmatically. Direct production changes risk lockouts (can't merge fixes). No testing creates surprises. Pilot testing + programmatic deployment enables safe protection rule rollout.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub organization has 50 teams working on shared microservices. Cross-team code ownership requires approval from multiple teams before merge.

options.label: CODEOWNERS with multiple owner entries and branch protection requiring all reviews

questions.correctLabel: CODEOWNERS with multiple owner entries and branch protection requiring all reviews
questions.extraHtml: CODEOWNERS supports multiple owners per path pattern (team-platform @team-a @team-b), branch protection can require reviews from all owners. Single owner misses stakeholder input. No requirements allow breaking changes. Multi-owner CODEOWNERS enables shared responsibility for critical components.

questions.promptHtml: Your CI/CD pipeline must optimize workflow execution time by sharing build artifacts between jobs without workspace duplication.

options.label: GitHub Actions upload-artifact and download-artifact actions

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions upload-artifact and download-artifact actions
questions.extraHtml: upload-artifact saves files after build job, download-artifact retrieves in subsequent jobs sharing artifacts efficiently. Rebuilding wastes time. External storage adds complexity. Built-in artifact sharing enables fast, cost-effective workflow optimization across jobs.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository needs automatic labeling of pull requests based on changed file paths with labels for frontend, backend, database, and documentation.

options.label: GitHub Actions with labeler action using path-based configuration

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with labeler action using path-based configuration
questions.extraHtml: Labeler action applies labels based on paths changed (src/frontend/* → frontend label), enables filtering and routing. Manual labeling is forgotten. No labels reduce PR discoverability. Automatic path-based labeling provides organized, searchable PR categorization.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization requires GitHub commits be immutable with commit signature verification required at deployment time preventing unsigned code in production.

options.label: Branch protection requiring signed commits and deployment workflow verifying signatures

questions.correctLabel: Branch protection requiring signed commits and deployment workflow verifying signatures
questions.extraHtml: Branch protection blocks unsigned commits, deployment workflow runs git verify-commit before deploying enforcing authenticity. Optional signing allows unsigned code. No verification misses impersonation. Mandatory signing + deployment verification ensures commit chain of custody.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub Actions workflow must deploy to Azure App Service with zero-downtime deployment using slot swaps after successful health checks.

options.label: GitHub Actions deploying to staging slot, health check, conditional slot swap

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions deploying to staging slot, health check, conditional slot swap
questions.extraHtml: Workflow deploys to staging slot, runs health tests against slot URL, swaps staging to production if healthy. Direct production deploys cause downtime. Manual swaps delay deployments. Automated slot swap with validation provides zero-downtime Azure deployments.

questions.promptHtml: Your security team needs GitHub Advanced Security findings automatically triaged with severity-based assignment to security engineers and product teams.

options.label: GitHub Actions processing security alerts via GraphQL API with routing logic

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions processing security alerts via GraphQL API with routing logic
questions.extraHtml: Scheduled workflow queries security alerts via GraphQL, evaluates severity/location/type, creates assigned issues to relevant teams with SLA tags. Manual triage doesn't scale and creates backlog. Email alerts create noise. Automated intelligent triage accelerates security issue resolution.

questions.promptHtml: A GitHub repository must maintain separate documentation versions for each product release with automatic deployment to GitHub Pages.

options.label: GitHub Actions with versioned docs folders and Pages deployment per tag/branch

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Actions with versioned docs folders and Pages deployment per tag/branch
questions.extraHtml: Workflow on tag creates versioned docs folder (docs/v1.0), builds all versions, deploys to Pages with version selector. Single version loses historical reference. Manual updates are error-prone and slow. Versioned docs automation maintains documentation for all supported product versions.

questions.promptHtml: Your organization needs GitHub Enterprise Server upgraded with zero downtime for global development teams requiring 24/7 availability.

options.label: GitHub Enterprise Server high-availability cluster with rolling upgrade

questions.correctLabel: GitHub Enterprise Server high-availability cluster with rolling upgrade
questions.extraHtml: HA cluster allows replica upgrade first, failover, then primary upgrade maintaining availability. Scheduled downtime disrupts global teams. Delaying upgrades misses security patches. HA rolling upgrades enable enterprise platform continuity during maintenance.
